title: Pickup stick patterns on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: Creating texture and float patterns by manually selecting warp threads with a pickup stick
excerpt: A pickup stick on a rigid heddle loom selects a subset of the down-shed warp threads to raise, creating a third shed that the rigid heddle alone cannot produce. This third shed allows simple supplementary warp float patterns (diamonds, chevrons, and spot motifs) without moving to a floor loom. A flat, smooth stick is the only additional tool needed.
sourceNotes: Pickup stick technique on a rigid heddle loom is documented in contemporary rigid heddle weaving instruction. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat, smooth, pointed stick used to manually select individual warp threads from the down-shed position and raise them, creating a third shed on a rigid heddle loom. A knitting needle or flat ruler will substitute in a short pick.
A weft or warp thread that passes over two or more adjacent threads rather than alternating under and over each one. Floats create surface texture; the pickup stick controls which warp threads float.
A woven surface pattern created by inserting a pickup stick through selected down-shed threads before passing the weft. The pattern depends entirely on which threads are picked up on each row.

content.text: A rigid heddle loom has two natural sheds: the up-shed (raised threads) and the down-shed (lowered threads). A
opens a third shed by manually lifting selected down-shed threads. The weft passes through this third shed and creates

items.name: Rigid heddle loom, warped at 8 or 10 EPI
Flat pickup stick (30 cm flat ruler, bone folder, or purpose-made stick)
Contrasting weft yarn (to show the pattern clearly)

content.text: Place the rigid heddle in the down position. The down-shed threads are the ones below the slot holes: the threads passing through the holes of the heddle are down. These are the threads the pickup stick will work with.
Step 2: Pick up the selected threads

With the heddle in the down position, insert the pickup stick from the right, working across the warp and picking up every other down-shed thread (or a pattern sequence such as pick up 3, skip 3, pick up 3). Slide the stick to the back of the loom but keep it in the warp.

Stand the pickup stick on its edge. This creates a shed between the picked-up threads and the unpicked down-shed threads. Pass the weft through this shed. The weft will float over the picked-up threads.
Step 4: Alternate with plain weave picks

After each pickup row, weave one or two plain weave rows (up-shed then down-shed alternating) before inserting the pickup stick again. This plain weave 'catches' the floats at each side and holds the pattern in the cloth. The pattern repeat depends on how often the pickup stick is used versus plain weave picks.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with pickup stick patterns.

items.symptom: Pattern shifts position row to row
items.cause: The pickup stick is selecting different threads each time it is inserted.
items.fix: Mark the first picked-up thread with a small tie of scrap yarn. Insert the stick to the same position each time.

items.symptom: Floats longer than intended on the cloth surface
items.cause: Too many threads picked up in a row without plain weave picks between pickup rows.
items.fix: Insert at least two plain weave picks between pickup pattern rows. Floats longer than 5 to 6 threads tend to snag in use.
